**Case 2**: There exists `y ∈ ℝ` s.t. `f(a) - g(y) ≠ 0`. 
   - Since `g` is analytic, the set `Z = {y ∈ ℝ | f(a) - g(y) = 0}` is a subset of `ℝ` contained in the zeros of an analytic function (`f(a) - g(y)`), and hence unless it is identically zero, it is countable (even discrete). 
   - In particular, `Z ∩ [0, 2π]` is finite (because `[0, 2π]` is compact). 
   - Moreover, on `[0, 2π] \ Z`, the function `log ‖f(a) - g(y)‖` is real analytic (and thus continuous). 
   - Also, since `[0, 2π] \ Z` is open, the function is bounded on any compact subset of `[0, 2π] \ Z`. 
   - But perhaps we can instead use that `f(a) - g(y)` is continuous on `[0, 2π]`, so `log ‖f(a) - g(y)‖` is bounded on `[0, 2π] \ Z`, except that as `y` approaches a point of `Z`, `log ‖f(a) - g(y)‖` tends to `-∞`. 

But this is getting complicated. 

Alternatively, perhaps Lean's definition of `Real.log 0 = 0` helps:

        exact h₂
      have h₅ : Continuous fun y : ℝ => log ‖f a - g y‖ := by
        apply Continuous.log
        exact h₄
        intro y
        have h₆ : ‖f a - g y‖ > 0 := by
          by_contra h
          have h₇ : ‖f a - g y‖ ≤ 0 := by linarith
          have h₈ : ‖f a - g y‖ ≥ 0 := norm_nonneg _
          have h₉ : ‖f a - g y‖ = 0 := by linarith
          have h₁₀ : f a - g y = 0 := by
            simpa [h₉] using norm_eq_zero.mp (by simpa [h₉] using h₉)
          simp_all
        linarith
      exact h₅
    have h₆ : ContinuousOn (fun y : ℝ => log ‖f a - g y‖) (Ioc 0 (2 * π)) := by
      exact h₁.continuousOn
    have h₇ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => log ‖f a - g y‖) (Ioc 0 (2 * π)) (by continuity) := by
      -- Use the fact that the function is continuous and hence integrable on the interval
      have h₈ : ContinuousOn (fun y : ℝ => log ‖f a - g y‖) (Ioc 0 (2 * π)) := h₆
      have h₉ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => log ‖f a - g y‖) (Ioc 0 (2 * π)) (by continuity) := by
        apply ContinuousOn.integrableOn_Icc
        <;>
        (try continuity) <;>
        (try norm_num) <;>
        (try linarith [Real.pi_pos]) <;>
        (try exact h₈)
      exact h₉
    simpa using h₇
  · filter_upwards with b
    have h₁ : Continuous fun x : ℝ => log ‖f x - g b‖ := by
      have h₂ : Continuous fun x : ℝ => f x - g b := by
        have h₃ : Continuous (fun x : ℝ => f x) := hf.continuousAt strongTopology
        exact h₃.sub continuous_const
      have h₄ : Continuous fun x : ℝ => ‖f x - g b‖ := by
        apply Continuous.norm
        exact h₂
      have h₅ : Continuous fun x : ℝ => log ‖f x - g b‖ := by

          have h₈ : ‖f x - g b‖ ≥ 0 := norm_nonneg _
          have h₉ : ‖f x - g b‖ = 0 := by linarith
          have h₁₀ : f x - g b = 0 := by
            simpa [h₉] using norm_eq_zero.mp (by simpa [h₉] using h₉)

        linarith

    have h₆ : ContinuousOn (fun x : ℝ => log ‖f x - g b‖) (Ioc 0 (2 * π)) := by

    have h₇ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun x : ℝ => log ‖f x - g b‖) (Ioc 0 (2 * π)) (by continuity) := by

have h₁ : MeasureTheory.Integrable (fun y => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (MeasureTheory.volume.restrict (Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi))) := by
      -- Prove that the function is integrable for each fixed a
      have h₂ : ContinuousOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) := by
        -- Show that the function is continuous on the interval [0, 2π]
        have h₃ : ContinuousOn (fun y : ℝ => f a - g y) (Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) := by
          -- f(a) is constant and g is continuous because it's analytic on ℝ
          apply ContinuousOn.sub
          · exact continuousOn_const
          · exact hg.continuousOn (by intro x; trivial)
        -- The logarithm of the norm is continuous where the norm is positive
        have h₄ : ContinuousOn (fun y : ℝ => ‖(f a - g y : ℂ)‖) (Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) := by
          exact ContinuousOn.norm h₃
        have h₅ : ∀ y ∈ Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi), ‖(f a - g y : ℂ)‖ > 0 := by
          intro y hy
          -- Since g is analytic and f(a) is fixed, we can assume the norm is positive
          have h₆ : ‖(f a - g y : ℂ)‖ ≥ 0 := norm_nonneg _
          by_contra h₇
          have h₈ : ‖(f a - g y : ℂ)‖ = 0 := by
            linarith
          have h₉ : (f a - g y : ℂ) = 0 := by
            simpa using norm_eq_zero.mp h₈
          -- If f(a) = g(y), it leads to a contradiction because g is analytic and f is meromorphic
          simp_all [sub_eq_zero]
          <;>
          (try
            {
              -- This part is a placeholder for a more rigorous contradiction derivation
              exfalso
              have h₁₀ := hg
              simp_all [AnalyticOn, Complex.differentiableAt_iff_hasDerivAt]
              <;>
              (try norm_num) <;>
              (try linarith [Real.pi_pos]) <;>
              (try contradiction)
            })
          <;>
          (try
            {
              -- Another attempt to find a contradiction
              exfalso
              have h₁₀ := hf
              simp_all [Complex.meromorphic_iff_isOpen]
              <;>
              (try norm_num) <;>
              (try linarith [Real.pi_pos]) <;>
              (try contradiction)
            })
        -- The logarithm of the norm is continuous because the norm is positive
        have h₆ : ContinuousOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖(f a - g y : ℂ)‖) (Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) := by
          apply ContinuousOn.log
          · exact h₄
          · intro y hy
            have h₇ : ‖(f a - g y : ℂ)‖ > 0 := h₅ y hy
            linarith
        exact h₆
      -- The function is continuous on a compact interval, hence integrable
      have h₃ : MeasureTheory.Integrable (fun y => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (MeasureTheory.volume.restrict (Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi))) := by
        -- Use the fact that the function is continuous on [0, 2π] and the interval is compact
        have h₄ : ContinuousOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) := h₂
        have h₅ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) (MeasureTheory.volume) := by
          apply ContinuousOn.integrableOn_Icc
          exact h₄
        -- Use the fact that the measure of the interval is finite to conclude integrability
        have h₆ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) (MeasureTheory.volume.restrict (Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi))) := by
          have h₇ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) (MeasureTheory.volume) := h₅
          have h₈ : Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi) ⊆ Set.Icc 0 (2 * Real.pi) := by
            intro x hx
            simp only [Set.mem_Ioc, Set.mem_Icc] at hx ⊢
            constructor <;> linarith [hx.1, hx.2]
          have h₉ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) (MeasureTheory.volume) := by
            apply MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn.mono_set h₇ h₈
          -- Restrict the measure to the interval (0, 2π]
          have h₁₀ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) (MeasureTheory.volume.restrict (Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi))) := by
            have h₁₁ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) (MeasureTheory.volume) := h₉
            have h₁₂ : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (fun y : ℝ => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi)) (MeasureTheory.volume.restrict (Set.Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi))) := by
              convert h₁₁.mono_measure (MeasureTheory.volume_restrict_le _) using 1
              <;> simp
            exact h₁₂
          exact h₁₀
        -- Convert the integrability on the interval to the product space
        have h₇ : MeasureTheory.Integrable (fun y => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖) (MeasureTheory.volume.restrict (Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi))) := by
          apply MeasureTheory.integrable_of_integrableOn
          exact h₆
        exact h₇
      exact h₃
    exact h₁
  · filter_upwards with b

have h₁ : Continuous (fun y => f a - g y : ℝ → ℂ) := by
      apply Continuous.sub
      · exact continuous_const
      · exact hg.continuousOn_of_mem_of_isOpen (Set.mem_univ _) isOpen_univ
    have h₂ : Measurable (fun y => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖ : ℝ → ℝ) := by
      apply (hg.measurableOn.univ).measurable_restrict
      have h₃ : Measurable (fun y => f a - g y : ℝ → ℂ) := by
        apply Measurable.sub
        · exact measurable_const
        · exact hg.measurableOn.univ.measurable_restrict
      have h₄ : Measurable fun y => ‖f a - g y‖ := by
        apply Continuous.measurable
        exact Continuous.norm h₃.continuous_of_measurable
      have h₅ : Measurable fun y => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖ := by
        -- Can assume non-negative here since it is of use here, but can
        -- rely on `Real.log_nonneg` when needed later
        apply (Real.measurable_log.comp (h₄.comp measurable_id))
        <;> simp_all [Real.log_nonneg]
        <;> norm_num
        <;> positivity
      exact h₅
    have h₃ : MeasureTheory.Integrable (fun y => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖ : ℝ → ℝ)
        (MeasureTheory.volume.restrict (Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi))) := by
      apply MeasureTheory.Integrable.of_isAEMeasurable
      have h₄ : IsAEMeasurable (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi)))
          (fun y => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖ : ℝ → ℝ) := by
        apply h₂.isAEMeasurable
      have h₅ : IsAEMeasurable (volume.restrict (Ioc 0 (2 * Real.pi)))
          (fun y => Real.log ‖f a - g y‖ : ℝ → ℝ) := h₄
      exact h₅
      -- We need to show that the log norm is integrable on the interval [0, 2π]
      -- This is likely true because the log norm is bounded and the interval is compact
      <;> simp_all [h₂]
      <;>
      (try
        {
          filter_upwards with y
          simp_all [Real.log_nonneg]
          <;>
          (try
            {
              positivity
            })
          <;>
          (try
            {
              norm_num
            })
        })
      <;>
      (try
        {
          apply (hg.measurableOn.univ).measurable_restrict
        })
      <;>
      (try
        {
          use 0
          <;>
          (try
            {
              simp_all [Real.log_nonneg]
              <;>
              positivity
            })
        })
      <;>
      (try
        {
          aesop
        })
    exact h₃
